claims.claim_text: AWS's February 2017 S3 disruption in us-east-1 was triggered when an authorized team member entered an incorrect value while removing servers from an S3 billing-related subsystem, affecting the index and placement subsystems.
claims.rationale: AWS's own incident summary directly describes the bad input, the affected S3 subsystems, and the resulting service disruption.

claims.claim_text: The outage took down large portions of the internet for nearly four hours and caused an estimated $150 million in impact across named companies and agencies.
claims.anchor_text: This simple human error triggered a cascading failure that took down massive portions of the internet for nearly four hours, resulting in an estimated 150 million dollars in financial impact across companies ranging from Slack to Trello to the Securities and Exchange Commission.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This operator error disrupted S3 in the Northern Virginia region for several hours and affected many services that depended on it.
claims.rationale: AWS supports the outage and its duration, but this sentence's dollar estimate and company list are not backed by the cited primary source.

claims.claim_text: AWS published a postmortem for the S3 event and said it added safeguards such as slower capacity removal and checks to prevent dropping subsystems below minimum required capacity.
claims.rationale: The AWS incident write-up explicitly lists the safeguard changes made after the event.

claims.claim_text: A text-based infrastructure template forces changes through version control, peer review, and automated preflight validation before production.
claims.anchor_text: When your infrastructure is defined explicitly in a text-based template file, changes are forced to go through version control, peer code review, and automated pre-flight validation before they ever touch a production environment.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When teams manage infrastructure as code, they can route changes through version control, code review, and template validation before production deployment.
claims.rationale: Those controls are workflow choices around IaC, not something CloudFormation itself universally forces.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation can roll stack operations back to a previous stable state when provisioning or update errors occur.
claims.rationale: AWS's CloudFormation documentation describes rollback behavior as a core safety control.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation manages over 750 resource types, new-service support usually arrives within weeks or on launch day, and the resource specification exceeds 80 MB uncompressed.
claims.anchor_text: - **CloudFormation manages over 750 distinct AWS resource types** as of 2026, covering virtually every service in the AWS ecosystem. When AWS launches a new service, CloudFormation support typically follows within weeks, often appearing on launch day. The full resource specification is published as a JSON schema that weighs in at over 80 megabytes uncompressed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **CloudFormation supports hundreds of AWS resource types**, and AWS publishes a machine-readable resource specification for them.
claims.rationale: The exact count, launch-timing generalization, and size figure are unstable or not directly supported on a primary AWS page.

claims.claim_text: A CloudFormation template can declare at most 500 resources, and larger architectures should be split across multiple templates such as nested stacks.
claims.rationale: The quotas page explicitly lists a 500-resource template limit and recommends splitting templates.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation drift detection launched in November 2018 and immediately flags manual console changes to managed resources.
claims.anchor_text: - **CloudFormation drift detection**, originally launched in November 2018, can definitively tell you when an administrator has manually changed a resource that CloudFormation manages. This solves the classic "who touched production?" problem; if a security group rule was added via the management console, drift detection flags the discrepancy immediately so you can decide whether to update the source template or revert the manual change.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **CloudFormation drift detection**, launched in November 2018, can report unmanaged changes on supported resources when you run a drift detection operation against a stack or resource.
claims.rationale: The launch date is supportable, but the current wording incorrectly implies continuous or immediate detection rather than an explicit drift check.

claims.claim_text: AWS CDK was introduced in July 2019 and produces a standard declarative YAML template under the hood.
claims.anchor_text: - **The AWS Cloud Development Kit**, introduced in July 2019, fundamentally generates CloudFormation templates under the hood. When you write infrastructure code in TypeScript, Python, or Go, the synthesizer command produces a standard declarative YAML template, meaning the kit is not a replacement for CloudFormation but rather a powerful higher-level authoring abstraction that compiles down to it.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **The AWS Cloud Development Kit**, which became generally available in July 2019, synthesizes CloudFormation templates under the hood. When you write infrastructure code in a supported language, the synth step produces a standard CloudFormation template, so CDK is a higher-level authoring layer rather than a replacement for CloudFormation.
claims.rationale: AWS supports July 2019 as the GA milestone and supports synthesis through CloudFormation, but the original wording overstates the date and template-format specifics.

claims.claim_text: The Resources section is the only required top-level section in a CloudFormation template; other sections are optional.
claims.rationale: AWS template anatomy documentation explicitly says Resources is required and describes the other sections as optional.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation uses logical IDs inside the template and, by default, generates unique physical resource names or IDs.
claims.span_hint: section: Resources: The Core of Every Template
claims.rationale: AWS's custom naming documentation explains that CloudFormation generates unique physical IDs when no custom name is supplied.

claims.claim_text: Using custom physical names can prevent replacement updates unless you specify a new name.
claims.span_hint: section: Resources: The Core of Every Template
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly warns that updates causing replacement are not supported for custom-named resources unless a new name is used.

claims.claim_text: AWS-specific CloudFormation parameter types validate against existing account and Region values, and the console can present them as dropdown selections.
claims.rationale: The AWS parameter-types guide states that these types catch invalid values and that the console prepopulates dropdown lists for many of them.

claims.claim_text: Using AllowedPattern guarantees exact CIDR validation for a networking value.
claims.anchor_text: Furthermore, using `AllowedPattern` with regular expressions guarantees that input data conforms exactly to expected formats, such as validating a networking CIDR block.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Furthermore, using `AllowedPattern` with regular expressions can enforce an expected string shape, but the pattern itself must be designed carefully if you want strict CIDR validation.
claims.rationale: CloudFormation supports regex validation, but the claim overstates what a given pattern inherently guarantees.

claims.claim_text: Fn::ImportValue imports exported outputs from another stack in the same Region, and the exporting stack cannot be deleted or have that export modified while it is in use.
claims.span_hint: section: Outputs: Sharing Information Between Stacks
claims.rationale: The ImportValue documentation explicitly states the same-Region restriction and the deletion/modification restriction once another stack imports the value.

claims.claim_text: Ref returns a parameter value or a resource's primary identifier, while GetAtt returns named resource attributes; for an EC2 instance, Ref returns the instance ID.
claims.span_hint: section: Intrinsic Functions: Ref and GetAtt
claims.rationale: AWS's Ref documentation and resource reference define these return-value semantics directly.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation determines resource operation order from explicit and implicit dependencies, including Ref, GetAtt, and Sub references.
claims.span_hint: section: Stack Lifecycle: Create, Update, Delete
claims.rationale: AWS's DependsOn documentation spells out the implicit dependency rules and resulting create, update, and delete ordering.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation documents resource-property update behavior as No interruption, Some interruption, or Replacement, and replacement usually creates the new resource first and then deletes the old one.
claims.rationale: AWS's update-behavior guide directly defines these categories and describes replacement sequencing.

claims.claim_text: Replacing an instance with an attached Elastic IP will immediately fail because the old instance still holds the exclusive lock on the Elastic IP.
claims.span_hint: section: Update Behavior > stop and think answer
claims.anchor_text: In this scenario, the Elastic IP is an exclusive resource that can only be associated with one running instance at a time. The "create-before-delete" process spins up the new instance and attempts to associate the Elastic IP to it while the old instance still holds the lock on that IP address. This causes a conflict, leading to an immediate stack update rollback. Handling exclusive dependencies requires careful template engineering.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In practice, replacement updates can fail when a dependent resource cannot be cleanly moved or recreated during the update, so you should review replacement behavior carefully for stateful or tightly coupled resources.
claims.rationale: The current explanation overstates a specific guaranteed failure mode instead of stating the broader, supportable replacement-risk principle.

claims.claim_text: Change sets let you preview how proposed stack changes would affect running resources, including whether a change may replace or delete a resource, before execution.
claims.span_hint: section: Change Sets: Preview Before You Apply
claims.rationale: AWS's change set documentation describes previewing impacts before deciding whether to execute the change set.

claims.claim_text: CloudFormation supports preserving successfully provisioned resources during failed stack operations, including via the CLI's disable-rollback behavior for create operations.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the preserve-successfully-provisioned-resources option and maps it to CLI failure-handling options.

claims.claim_text: Nested stacks are declared with AWS::CloudFormation::Stack, and parent templates can read nested outputs using GetAtt with the Outputs.OutputName form.
claims.rationale: AWS's nested-stack resource reference explicitly documents both the resource type and the Outputs access pattern.

claims.claim_text: Terraform uses a state file and surfaces unmanaged changes as drift in future plans.
claims.rationale: HashiCorp's drift documentation explicitly describes Terraform state as a file and explains that out-of-band changes appear in future plan diffs.

claims.claim_text: AWS governance services such as Service Catalog and Control Tower rely on CloudFormation templates or StackSets.
claims.span_hint: section: CloudFormation vs Terraform > Use CloudFormation exclusively when
claims.rationale: AWS documentation states that Service Catalog products are based on CloudFormation templates and that Control Tower uses CloudFormation StackSets.

claims.claim_text: AWS CDK supports multiple general-purpose languages and provisions infrastructure through CloudFormation.
claims.rationale: The AWS CDK guide lists the supported languages and says CDK provisions infrastructure through CloudFormation.

claims.claim_text: The example is a 20-line Python class that expands into a CloudFormation template with a VPC, six subnets, route tables, a NAT Gateway, and an Internet Gateway, while hand-written YAML would exceed 200 lines.
claims.anchor_text: This remarkably concise 20-line Python class effectively generates an extensive CloudFormation template containing an entire VPC, six independent subnets correctly distributed across three availability zones, associated route tables, a managed NAT Gateway, and an Internet Gateway. Manually writing this would easily exceed 200 lines of verbose YAML.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This concise Python class expands into a much more verbose CloudFormation template for a multi-AZ VPC and its supporting network resources.
claims.rationale: The exact line counts are illustrative rather than primary-source facts and should be removed.

claims.claim_text: When CDK deployments fail, the resulting errors exclusively reference underlying CloudFormation logical IDs and rules.
claims.anchor_text: When a CDK deployment inevitably fails, the resulting stack trace and error logs exclusively reference the underlying CloudFormation engine, its logical IDs, and its rigid declarative rules.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a CDK deployment fails during provisioning, you often need to inspect the underlying CloudFormation stack events and logical IDs to diagnose it.
claims.rationale: The strong word "exclusively" overstates the debugging surface; the teachable point is that CloudFormation remains an important layer to inspect.

claims.claim_text: Named IAM resources in CloudFormation require CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM during stack creation or update.
claims.rationale: The CreateStack API reference explicitly states that custom-named IAM resources require CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table's cost row states CloudFormation is free and Terraform is free except for Terraform Cloud.
claims.anchor_text: | **Cost** | Free | Free (OSS), paid for Terraform Cloud |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Cost** | AWS resource charges apply, and some CloudFormation extension operations can add service charges | Terraform CLI use is broadly free, while managed HashiCorp offerings add paid tiers |
claims.rationale: The original row is too absolute: CloudFormation has pricing nuances, and HashiCorp offers multiple paid managed tiers beyond the narrow Terraform Cloud phrasing.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table states CloudFormation is slower because it is sequential by default, while Terraform is faster because it is parallel by default.
claims.anchor_text: | **Speed** | Slower (sequential by default) | Faster (parallel by default) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Speed** | Execution speed depends heavily on the resources involved and their dependency graph | Execution speed depends heavily on provider behavior and the dependency graph |
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly says CloudFormation parallelizes independent work where possible, so the original row's blanket sequential-vs-parallel framing is not supportable.

further_reading.title: CloudFormation Template Sections
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical reference for what each top-level template section does and which sections are required.

further_reading.title: Update Stacks Using Change Sets
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers the safest operational workflow for previewing updates, replacements, and deletes before execution.

further_reading.title: Split Templates with Nested Stacks
further_reading.why_relevant: It shows how to decompose larger CloudFormation architectures once a single template becomes hard to manage.

notes: Quiz items, code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded per the task rules. The comparison table was only flagged where it makes unstable, pricing, or unsupported benchmark claims.
